planting.note: Sow direct in fall for cold stratification and spring germination, or direct sow in early spring; bluestem goldenrod is one of the very few native goldenrods that thrives in significant shade, making it invaluable for shaded pollinator gardens.

directions: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination, or provide 4–6 weeks of cold stratification before spring sowing; bluestem goldenrod grows in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil and is among the most shade-tolerant of all goldenrods. Solidago caesia is an eastern North American native goldenrod of deciduous forest interiors and edges producing slender, arching, bluish-green stems bearing clusters of small, bright-yellow flowers nestled in the leaf axils along the arching stem length (not in a large terminal plume like most goldenrods) from late summer through fall, creating an elegant, arching spray of yellow that is invaluable for late-season pollinator support in shaded gardens. Unlike most goldenrods that require full sun, bluestem goldenrod is specifically adapted to woodland shade and is an outstanding choice for the transition zone between sunny and shaded garden areas; it is an excellent companion for native wood asters, great Solomon's seal, and native ferns.

planting.note: Sow direct in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; zigzag goldenrod is the most shade-adapted of all native goldenrods and grows naturally in the understory of mature deciduous forests.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; zigzag goldenrod is specifically adapted to woodland interior shade and is one of the finest native goldenrods for true shade gardens alongside ferns, Solomon's seal, and shade-tolerant asters. Solidago flexicaulis is named for its distinctively zigzagging stem that changes direction at each leaf node — a unique and charming characteristic; it produces broad, toothed, oval leaves (broader than most goldenrods) and clusters of small, bright-yellow flowers at the leaf axils along the zigzag stem in late summer and fall. Like bluestem goldenrod (S. caesia), it provides critical late-season nectar for pollinators in shaded woodland gardens where few other plants are in bloom at that time; it spreads slowly by rhizomes to form loose, naturalistic colonies under deciduous trees.

planting.note: Sow direct in fall or early spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; wrinkleleaf goldenrod tolerates consistently wet soil and is one of the best goldenrods for rain gardens, pond margins, and moist meadows.

directions: Direct sow in fall or provide 4–6 weeks of cold stratification before spring sowing; wrinkleleaf goldenrod thrives in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade and is one of the best native goldenrods for persistently wet, difficult sites. Solidago rugosa is a robust, upright to arching eastern North American goldenrod with wrinkled, rugose (rough and deeply veined) leaves and arching, one-sided racemes of bright yellow flowers in late summer through fall; it tolerates consistently wet soil better than most goldenrods, making it valuable for rain gardens, pond margins, and moist meadow restorations. The cultivar 'Fireworks' is a spectacular garden selection with strongly arching, fountain-like flowering branches creating a dramatic display; it spreads vigorously by rhizomes in moist, fertile soils. Outstanding late-season pollinator plant for monarchs, bees, and wasps.

planting.note: Sow direct in fall for cold stratification and spring germination; cup plant can self-seed aggressively in fertile, moist soils — dead-head promptly after blooming if you wish to limit spreading in smaller gardens.

directions: Direct sow in fall for cold stratification and spring germination, or start indoors with 6–8 weeks of cold stratification; cup plant is a large, aggressive colonizer — plant it where there is space for it to naturalize, not in small, confined garden beds. Silphium perfoliatum is one of the most bold and architecturally striking native prairie plants of eastern North America, growing 5–8 feet tall with stout, square stems and distinctive opposite leaves that are perfoliate (fused at the base to form a cup around the stem) — these cups collect rainwater that provides drinking water for birds and small insects throughout summer. In mid- to late summer it produces an abundance of 2–3-inch, yellow, sunflower-like flowers with prominent yellow rays and dark centers that are magnets for native bees, goldfinches, and many butterfly species. The cup-forming leaves and tall stature make it one of the most distinctive North American prairie plants and a great choice for naturalized areas.

planting.note: Sow direct in fall for cold stratification and spring germination, or plant corm divisions in spring 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; prairie blazing star is drought-tolerant once established but needs good drainage.

directions: Sow direct in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination, or plant corm divisions in spring 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean soil in full sun; prairie blazing star requires excellent drainage and is best in open, dry, prairie-style plantings. Liatris pycnostachya is the tall, densely flowered prairie blazing star of the central US tall-grass prairie, producing erect, unbranched spikes of deep rose-purple, fuzzy button flowers from the top of the spike down (the reverse of most flowers) from mid-summer through fall — one of the most intensely colored and prolific native prairie flowers for full sun. Like all liatris, it is an outstanding monarch butterfly nectar plant during their fall migration, and the seeds are an important food source for goldfinches and other seed-eating birds in winter. Outstanding in prairie restorations, sunny native plant gardens, and as a cut flower.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; New York ironweed tolerates consistently wet soils and is excellent in rain gardens, moist meadows, and along streams.

directions: Sow direct in spring or start indoors with 4–6 weeks of cold stratification; New York ironweed prefers moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade and is one of the best native perennials for consistently wet, sunny sites. Vernonia noveboracensis is a magnificent, tall (4–7 feet), upright native perennial of eastern North American moist meadows, stream margins, and wet prairies; it produces narrow, lance-shaped, toothed leaves on stout stems and bears large, flat-topped clusters of brilliant, deep royal-purple, fluffy, disc flowers (no ray petals) in late summer through fall — one of the most intensely saturated purples in the native plant palette and a standout in late-season gardens. The flowers are exceptional late-season nectar sources for monarchs, swallowtails, and native bees; the persistent, reddish-brown seed heads provide winter interest. Name does not restrict its range — it occurs throughout much of the eastern and central US.
